cards.text: A {{c1::forward declaration}} tells the compiler about the {{c2::existence}} of an identifier before it is {{c3::fully defined}}, allowing it to be used before its full definition.
A forward declaration for a function consists of the function's {{c1::return type}}, {{c2::name}}, and {{c3::parameter types}}, followed by a {{c4::semicolon}}.
A forward declaration is also called a function {{c1::prototype}} or function {{c2::declaration}}.

cards.question: Which is the forward declaration?
cards.code:
int add(int x, int y); // A

int add(int x, int y) { // B
return x + y;
}
cards.answer: A is the forward declaration (prototype). B is the function definition.
cards.explanation: The forward declaration ends with a semicolon and has no body. The definition includes the function body.

cards.front: Why would you need a forward declaration?
cards.back:
Forward declarations allow you to:
  • Call a function before it's defined
  • Resolve circular dependencies
  • Organize code with definitions after main()
  • Separate interface from implementation

cards.question: Will this code compile without a forward declaration?
cards.code:
int main() {
printValue(5); // Call before definition
return 0;
}

void printValue(int x) {
std::cout << x;
}
cards.answer: No. The compiler doesn't know about printValue() when main() tries to call it. A forward declaration is needed.
cards.explanation: Without a forward declaration, the compiler encounters the call to printValue before knowing it exists.

cards.text: A function {{c1::declaration}} tells the compiler about a function's existence. A function {{c2::definition}} provides the actual implementation/body.

cards.question: Write a forward declaration for: int multiply(int a, int b)
cards.explanation: The forward declaration is the function signature followed by a semicolon, no body needed.

cards.text: Parameter {{c1::names}} do {{c2::not}} need to match between forward declaration and definition. Only the parameter {{c3::types}} need to match.

cards.question: Are these forward declaration and definition compatible?
cards.code:
int add(int x, int y); // Forward declaration

int add(int a, int b) { // Definition
return a + b;
}
cards.answer: Yes, they are compatible. Parameter names don't need to match, only types and return type.
cards.explanation: The compiler only cares that the types match (int, int) and return type matches (int).

Use forward declarations when:
  • You want to define functions after main()
  • Two functions call each other (mutual recursion)
  • You want to organize code logically
  • Separating interface from implementation
However, if a function is defined before it's used, no forward declaration is needed.

cards.front: Can a function have multiple declarations but only one definition?
cards.back: Yes. A function can be declared multiple times (though usually not needed) but must have exactly one definition (One Definition Rule).

cards.text: Forward declarations are typically placed at the {{c1::top}} of the source file, before {{c2::main()}} or other functions that use them.
